case_name: Terexotic — Terwish — Tereros — Terseries — Tersanne — Tervirgin
parties: Terra Nigra Holding B.V. v CPVO
title: CPVO BoA A008-013/2002 - Terexotic — Terwish — Tereros — Terseries — Tersanne — Tervirgin
text:
European Union
Community Plant Variety Office
Cases A 008 - 013/2002
DECISION
Concerning the Appeals lodged by
TERRA NIGRA Holding B.V., Iepenlaan 48, 1424 PA De Kwakel, The Netherlands,
Representative: P.E. Boerlage
further party to the proceedings
Community Plant Variety Office
Representative: President Bart Kiewiet
Relating to Community plant variety rights EU 5082,5083,5085, 5245,5246,5248
Variety denominations:
TEREXOTIC,TERWISH, TEREROS,TERSERIES,TERSANNE, TERVIRGIN
Species: Gerbera L.
On 15 May 2003, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed
of Gabriele Winkler (Chairman), Henrik Wanscher and Professor Dr. Hanns Ullrich
(Members),
gave the following decision:
The appeals are rejected

- 2 -
Facts
The appellant is the holder of around 100 Community plant variety rights. On
25 October 1999 it was granted the six plant variety rights subject to proceedings in
respect of plant varieties of the species Gerbera L.
By letter of 11 September 2000 the Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter: CPVO)
reminded the holder of the rights that the second annual fees for the individually listed
rights were due on 30 November 2000. At the same time it was pointed out that a
surcharge of 20% would be levied on the annual fees if they were not paid by the due
date. Invoices relating to the second annual fees each in the amount of 500 € were
attached to the letter.
Since payment was not made in due time, the CPVO levied, by letter of
11 December 2000, surcharges on the second annual fees each in the amount of 100 €
and announced that the plant varieties rights would be cancelled if the second annual
fees plus surcharges were not paid by 11 January 2001. The holder of the rights also
allowed this time limit to pass.
The same procedure was repeated in respect of the third annual fees. By letter of
17 September 2001 the CPVO reminded the holder of the rights that the third annual
fees for the individually listed rights were due on 30 November 2001. At the same time it
was pointed out that a surcharge of 20% would be levied on the annual fees if they were
not paid by the due date. Invoices relating to the third annual fees each in the amount of
600 €were attached to the letter.
On 29 November 2001 the holder of the rights transferred 6,597.01 € to the CPVO
without stating the rights for which the money was intended. At that time it owed the
CPVO around 50,000 € for the maintenance of the six plant variety rights subject to
proceedings and a large number of other such rights.
An inquiry as to the relevant purpose made by the CPVO on 4 December 2001 went
unanswered.
By letter of 20 December 2001 the CPVO levied surcharges on the third annual fees
each in the amount of 120 € and announced that the plant variety rights would be

- 3 -
cancelled if the outstanding debts were not discharged by 20 January 2002. The holder
of the rights also allowed this time limit to pass.
By two letters of 25 January 2002 a detailed overall statement of account relating to all
due annual fees and surcharges was drawn up in respect of the holder of the rights. In an
attached list each individual right, the amount owed in respect thereof and the reason for
the debt was set out. The overall debt, including that in respect of the plant variety rights
subject to proceedings, amounted to 20,902.99 € (letter 1) and 29,200 € (letter 2). The
transfer by the holder of the rights on 29 November 2001 was taken into account as a
credit. In these letters it was pointed out that the listed plant variety rights would be
cancelled if the debts were not extinguished within a one-month period commencing ten
days after the date of posting. The letters were served on the holder of the rights by
registered post on 29 January 2002. It took no action.
By decisions of 15 April 2002 the six plant variety rights subject to proceedings - and
41 others - were cancelled on account of failure to pay the second and third annual fees
and the surcharges thereon.
No appeal was lodged against 37 decisions.
On 13 June 2002 the holder of the rights lodged by fax appeals against the other
10 decisions, including those relating to the rights subject to proceedings, which it
confirmed shortly thereafter by presenting the originals.
On 14 June 2002 the CPVO received a letter from the appellant in which it allocated the
transferred amount of 6,597 € as follows: 317 € related to fees other than those in
question here; 2,680 € were intended for maintenance of the plant variety rights in
respect of the varieties Selcoulomb, Selaunum, Tergrizzle and Terspits. The decisions
of the CPVO cancelling the plant variety rights in respect of these four rights were then
annulled by way of interlocutory revision.
The remaining 3,600 €were to cover the second annual fees plus surcharges in respect
of the six rights subject to proceedings.
Since 4,320 € were th erefore still outstanding in respect of the six third annual fees plus
surcharges, the competent committee of the CPVO did not grant interlocutory revision
and remitted these proceedings to the Board of Appeal for a decision.

- 4 -
The appellant contends that it paid the second annual fees plus surcharges by stating
the purpose of its transfer of 29 November 2001.
It requests that
the contested decisions of the CPVO of 15 April 2002 be annulled and that the
plant variety rights in respect of TEREXOTIC, TERWISH, TEREROS,
TERSERIES, TERSANNE and TERVIRGIN be maintained.
The CPVO requests that
the appeals be dismissed.
It sees no ground for annulling the contested decisions. It is true that the CPVO is not
compelled to cancel plant variety rights in the event of delay in payment since
Article 21(2) of Council Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 of 27 July 1994 on Community
plant variety rights (hereinafter: CR 2100) is an optional provision. However, in the
present case there are no apparent circumstances which justify maintaining the rights.
The holder of the rights now owes the CPVO more than 60,000 € for maintenance of a
large number of plant variety rights. Furthermore, it has not deemed it necessary to take
note of the contents of the registered letters and decisions. It has failed to notice that it
also owes the third annual fees plus surcharges.
Grounds
The admissible appeals are unfounded since the appellant has no entitlement to
maintenance of the plant variety rights and annulment of the contested decisions.
Under Article 21(2)(c) CR 2100, the Office may cancel a Community plant variety right
with effect in futurum if the holder, after being requested to do so, and within a time limit
specified by the Office, fails to pay such fees as may be payable to keep the Community
plant variety right in force.

- 5 -
This condition has been complied with. The appellant did not pay the second annual
fees plus surcharges until after the time limit and has still not paid the third annual fees
plus surcharges.
The fees were due and payment thereof by 4 March 2002 was duly requested.
Under Article 9(1) of Commission Regulation (EC) No 1238/95 of 31 May 1995
establishing implementing rules for the application of Council Regulation (EC)
No 2100/94 as regards the fees payable to the Community Plant Variety Office
(hereinafter: CR 1238), the Office is to charge a holder of a Community plant variety
right a fee for each year of the duration of a Community plant variety right (annual fee) as
laid down in Annex II.
Under paragraph 2, the date for payment of the annual fee is to be the last day of the
calendar month following the calendar month in which the Community plant variety right
was granted, and in each subsequent year on the corresponding day.
Since the plant variety rights were granted on 25 October 1999, the second annual fees
were due for payment on 30 November 2000 and the third annual fees on
30 November 2001.
Under Article 13(2)(a) CR 1238, the Office may levy a surcharge to the annual fee if it
establishes that the holder has failed to pay the annual fee in accordance with
Article 9(2) and (3).
The conditions for levying the surcharges have been complied with. The CPVO correctly
established that the second and third annual fees were not paid by the relevant due
dates. The amount transferred on 29 November 2001 could not extinguish the debt in
respect of the second annual fees and the surcharges thereon until the purpose was
stated on 14 June 2002.
In accordance with the requirements laid down in Article 9(3) CR 1238, the CPVO
stated the subject of payment, the amount due in each case and the date for payment,
and provided information on the possibility of surcharges, in respect of each individual
right by the abovementioned letters of 11 September 2000 and 17 September 2001.

- 6 -
The holder of the rights failed to observe the period for payment that it was finally
allowed, namely the period ending 4 March 2002. This period began to run effectively.
By serving the requests for payment by registered letter, the CPVO fulfilled its obligation
under Article 79 CR 2100 to serve notifications from which a time limit is reckoned.
A period of one month commencing 10 days after the posting of the registered letters
was allowed, that is to say until 4 March 2002. The letters were served on
29 January 2002. Therefore, the holder of the rights had more than the one-month period
allowed under Article 70 of Commission Regulation (EC) No 1239/95 of 31 May 1995
establishing implementing rules for the application of Council Regulation (EC)
No 2100/94 as regards proceedings before the Community Plant Variety Office
(hereinafter: CR 1239) within which to act.
Since the second annual fees plus surcharges were not paid until after time limit of
14 June 2002 and the third annual fees plus surcharges where not paid at all, there is no
entitlement to maintenance of the plant variety right and annulment of the contested
decisions. The appellant’s belief that the overall debt in respect of the rights subject to
proceedings had been extinguished by the (late) payment of the second annual fees
plus surcharges is clearly due to the fact that it failed to take note of the contents either
of the registered letters or the contested decisions which also related unequivocally to
the due date of the third annual fees plus surcharges. The statement, contrary to its
interests, of the purpose of the partial amount of 3,600 €can probably only be explained
thus. If it had been aware of the situation, the appellant could have used 2,680 €to pay
both the second and third annual fees plus surcharges in respect of two of the six rights
subject to proceedings and also have obtained a decision on interlocutory revision in
that respect. However, since he allocated this amount of 3,600 € to the second annual
fees plus surcharges, the debt on none of these rights was extinguished in full.
The appellant failed to observe the time limit for oral proceedings. It was duly invited.
Therefore, the question may remain open as to whether or not the CPVO could have
given it an opportunity to change the stated purpose with the consequence that at least
two of the six rights subject to proceedings could have remained in force.
Nor does the fact that Article 21(2) CR 2100 is an optional provision, which leaves the
cancellation of a plant variety right in the case of delay in payment to the discretion of the

- 7 -
CPVO, mean that the contested decisions must be annulled. As a rule, this provision
assumes that a plant variety right will be cancelled where the conditions therefor are
complied with. However, the optional nature of the provision allows the CPVO not to
cancel the right under particular circumstances where it is equitable to do so. There are
no such circumstances in this case. The CPVO was extremely lenient with the holder of
the rights in that it allowed it several periods, did not lay down a preclusive period for
stating the relevant purpose pursuant to Article 5(2) CR 1238, accepted as late as
June 2002 the statement of the purpose of the transfer of 29 November 2001 as
payment in respect of the plant variety rights which had already been cancelled by the
relevant decisions, and rectified the appeals in four cases. By contrast, the holder of the
rights, who has sufficient experience of matters relating to plant variety rights,
disregarded the contents of a large number of (registered) letters, invoices and
decisions and failed to display the diligence to be expected of it as a trader and a party
to the proceedings. Its payment behaviour in relation to its high level of debt with the
CPVO, by which it gave rise to levies of execution against it, also precludes the CPVO
from exercising discretion in its favour. Since the plant variety rights can be cancelled
only with effect in futurum, all fees and surcharges due prior to cancellation are owed
even if the cancellation of the plant variety rights becomes final.
Winkler Wanscher
